facts_disclosed: Plans to stay in Dubai permanently — sees herself here forever, not going back to Brazil, better opportunities

turns.content: I had the most interesting conversation with a colleague today and it's got me thinking. Do you have a minute?
Of course! What happened? I'm all ears.

So one of the other PTs at my clinic — she's from the Philippines — she told me she's moving back home at the end of the year. She's been here eight years and she said she always planned to save up and go back eventually. And everyone at the clinic was like "oh that makes sense, everyone leaves Dubai eventually" and I was sitting there thinking... do they?? Because I genuinely don't plan to leave.

That's an interesting reaction. A lot of expats in Dubai do treat it as a temporary chapter — save money, build experience, then go home. But it's definitely not universal. What makes you feel differently about it?

Honestly? I see myself here forever. Like truly, permanently. I know that sounds dramatic for someone from Brazil but when I really think about what my life would look like if I went back to Sao Paulo... ai meu Deus, the salary alone. I would make maybe a third of what I make here. And the professional opportunities? In Dubai I work with professional athletes, elite sports patients, people from all over the world. In Brazil I'd be working in a public clinic doing knee replacements on vovós. No disrespect to vovós but that is not my passion haha.

Those are really concrete reasons — the financial difference between PT salaries in Brazil versus the UAE is significant, and working with high-level athletes is a career niche that's hard to replicate elsewhere. Do your friends and family back in Brazil understand your decision, or do you get pressure to come home?

Oh gente, you have no idea. My tias — my aunties — every time I video-call it's "Bruna when are you coming back?" "Bruna your mother misses you!" "Bruna you're going to get old alone in that desert!" Haha they mean well but they don't understand. They've never left Sao Paulo, they don't know what it's like to have the kind of career growth I have here. And my mom gets it — she's sad but she gets it. She knows I'm not going back.

It sounds like your mom is supportive even though it's hard for her — that's really valuable. The pressure from extended family is so common for expats, especially from close-knit cultures. Have you thought about the long-term logistics of staying permanently though? Things like retirement planning, healthcare as you age, residency status?

Yes! This is actually something I've been researching. The UAE has this new golden visa system — you can get 10-year residency now and there's talk of making it easier for skilled professionals. I'm looking into it. And I save aggressively, like I put away 30% of my salary every month because there's no income tax here which is INSANE. Try doing that in Brazil where the government takes everything haha.

The golden visa is a smart path to look into — the 10-year residency removes a lot of the instability that makes people treat Dubai as temporary. And saving 30% with no income tax is genuinely building wealth at a pace that would be nearly impossible in most countries. You're clearly thinking about this pragmatically, not just emotionally.

What about the social side though? A lot of long-term Dubai expats talk about the revolving door — friends leave, you rebuild your circle, they leave again. Does that bother you?

Okay that part is real and it does hurt sometimes. I've lost count of how many farewell brunches I've been to haha. You get close to someone and then they get transferred or their visa situation changes or they just decide to go home. But you know what? That happens everywhere. In Sao Paulo people move to different cities, they change jobs, they drift apart. At least here the people who stay are the ones who really CHOSE to be here, and those friendships feel deeper to me.

That's a really mature perspective — the friendships forged by people who've all made the deliberate choice to stay tend to have a different quality. You're building a chosen community rather than just defaulting to proximity.

It sounds like you've genuinely weighed this from multiple angles — career, finances, social life, family dynamics, legal residency. Some people drift into permanent expat life without thinking about it, but you seem very intentional about it.

I am! And look, do I miss Brazil? Every single day meu bem. The food, the energy, Carnival, speaking Portuguese with everyone, the beach... I get saudade so bad sometimes I cry haha. But missing something doesn't mean you should go back to it. I can visit Brazil anytime. I go back twice a year, I eat all the pao de queijo and feijoada I want, I hug my mom, and then I come back to MY life here. This is home now.

That last line says everything — "this is home now." You can hold saudade for Brazil and still know that Dubai is where your life is. Those biannual trips sound like they give you enough connection to recharge without pulling you back permanently. You've clearly made peace with this being a both/and situation rather than an either/or.

Exactly!! Both/and, I love that. I'm Brazilian in my heart forever but Dubai is my home. I think my colleague leaving just made me realize how clear I am about this. Some people are here temporarily, and that's fine. But I'm not one of them. Okay I feel very emotional now haha, obrigada for listening! I needed to say all that out loud.

Sometimes you need a moment like your colleague's departure to crystallize what you already know about yourself. You've clearly thought this through deeply, and the fact that you can hold homesickness and certainty at the same time shows a lot of self-awareness. Dubai is lucky to have you — and your patients definitely are. Thanks for sharing all of that.
